speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 11, 1995. The survivor is David Jakubowski, J-A-K-U-B-O-W-S-K-I. And this is Riviera Beach, Florida, tape number 3.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, a German SS soldier brought to our ambulatory an infant, two to three weeks old. And he said, take care of him. So I put the baby-- I told the nurse, put the baby in the small room and give him something to drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember that next day came in an officer of the SS, a tall guy. He was probably 6' 2', 6' 3'. And he was asked, who is in charge? I said, I am in charge. So he looked around. And he went in the small room and found the baby. Oh! He got so angry, so mad. He started screaming, what is this? So I said-- in German, I knew German very well-- I said, sir, a German soldier brought the baby to take care of it. And he started to screaming, this is a Jew! This is one! Take him immediately out to the trains! So I asked one the nurses to go out with me to the train. And I took the baby. And this is the first time I was out on the Umschlagplatz to see how they're loading, what are they doing there. To describe this is beyond any imagination.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, Dante wrote This is worse than hell. This was worse than hell. The screaming, the pushing, the beating, the shooting, the killing, the wild dogs tearing people apart-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I will never get rid of it. [CRYING] We gave the baby-- they were pushing 200 people into one wagon-- into one car, where only 50 or 60 maximum could get in. And I remember the nurse gave the baby to one lady standing in front. And then suddenly, I saw from the left side approaching us, running, around 25 or 30 yeshiva students. This was students in the rabbinical school in black hat, black coats, white socks, you know, and, and those shoes-- black shoes. They were running. And behind them, a few SS men with machine guns-- hand-machine guns. And I said to the nurse, please, stay here. Wait for me. I want to see what they're going to do. They run, then, probably another hundred yards-- put them all over-- all at the wall-- there was a wall of a building-- in one line, and machine-gunned them right down, all of them. Now, this was a little too much for me. I couldn't take it anymore. I was close to a nervous breakdown. And I went to the president of Society Ganz. And I said, I can't do it anymore. I am resigning. This was three weeks-- this was in August. And I went back to the hospital. And I was working on the obstetrics. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was working by myself at nights only. With me was an old Jewish midwife, 75 years old. I was a young man at that time. And we're doing mostly deliveries, and mostly peculiar, during the night. I could do in one night 12 deliveries-- deliver 12 babies. Now we were trained to do everything. Today, if there is any complication, they do right away a Cesarean section. At that time, I was doing breaches, turning around, everything. But, one thing interesting. They didn't give us any gloves, rubber gloves. And we didn't have any soap. They were giving us pieces of soap. It was like stone. I couldn't clean my hands with this.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what was I doing? Just, under the water, washed the arms, the forearms, the hands from the blood. And the midwife was just putting iodine from the elbows down to the ends of my fingers. And that's how I was delivering one after other, 12-- one-- a few-- within few hours during the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: And around 5 o'clock-- 5:00, 6:00-- I was lying down to rest an hour or two. And interesting, when I was getting up around 8 o'clock, there were 10 to 12 babies in the room, but not one mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I repeat, not one mother. They left, ran out. The next day after delivery, they run out and left the babies there. Now, naturally the Germans were coming and taking the babies out to the Umschlagplatz. Or the babies died in the hospital because nobody could feed them. There was no medic. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is something which is unforgettable to me. Now, the so-called resettlement was still going on. Was more and more difficult to get the 6,000 people to the Umschlagplatz. Doesn't matter who-- man, woman, children-- 6,000. So, what the German did, they said, whoever will come voluntarily to the Umschlagplatz will get a bread and jam and butter. Some went. Because at that time, nobody believed. We were so naive-- we were so naive that nobody believed that they are going to death. We didn't know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Interesting thing happened there. The Polish people who were waiting on the trains there-- there were Polish people there. And they knew me. They came to me. And they are telling me an interesting-- they were telling me an interesting story, that the trains were leaving late afternoon, and the same cars with the same numbers were back next morning. So they couldn't go any far-- very far. Somewhere close, because all of them-- the same numbers cars-- were back in the morning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But this was going on on September 7, 1942. That was supposed to be the last day of the resettlement. Now, 10,000 people were supposed to go to be delivered to the Umschlagplatz. The rest-- 3,000 Judenrat worker, with their wives-- could stay; the whole Jewish police with their wives; and the people who were working in Tebbles-- Toebbens-- Toebbens was a German factory. They were producing parts for the war machine-- for the German war machine-- was Toebbens and Bauer, two Germans. So this was around 30,000 people. Now, those who were staying were getting numbers, life numbers-- who get a life number could stay. I was on the second floor of Judenrat because I knew everybody there. I didn't have a number. I was ready to go. I looked out-- unforgettable-- but I looked out. And there was a big huge square in front of the-- of the Judenrat. There were Jews, Orthodox Jews, praying there, and singing psalms. [CRYING] So-- [MOAN] sorry. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: In a moment, she came up, Mrs. Remba, and she saw me. As I said, I was working with her on the-- and she asked me, David, do you have a number? I said no, I'm going. [SIGHS] She said, don't move, stay here. So I said all right, I'll stay here. She went into the president's room. There was no Czerniakow there. So was some other one. And two minutes later, Nachum Remba came out, pale. They looked at me, you don't have a number? I said no. Stay here, don't move.

speaker: interviewee
text: One minute later, he comes out with a number. He told me what happened. He went-- he asked a guy, who was after Czerniakow. And he said, I have to have a number for a young physician. He says, but I have only a few for my family. He grabbed one ticket out from his hand and brought it to me. This was the second miracle. I went right down and mixed with the, the people who were working. There were 3,000 for the Judenrat. And now I survived the second time. Now, after September 7th, the ghetto was much smaller. I will show you the ghetto. I have a picture of it. It was the large one and the small one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They cut the small one off completely. To get from the big one to the small one, we had to go through a bridge over Zelazna Street. Otherwise, we couldn't get there because the other one was the Aryan, the Polish, the, the car, the trucks-- the trucks and the-- the-- not the trains-- the trains were going in there, too. But we had to go through the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Bridge over the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a bridge over the street. And the bridge was naturally liquidated. Now, in the small ghetto, it was easy to find an apartment. Because, as I said, a hundred thousand died from starvation and disease. So 400,000 were left in the ghetto. 370,000 were moved. We didn't know where.

speaker: interviewee
text: So 30,000 were left. So everybody had-- I got an apartment on Muranowska Street. I got an apartment, five rooms, beautiful furniture. I was alone in it. Next to me lived a friend of mine. He was an obstetrician-gynecologist. He had an apartment-- five rooms, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So everybody had to work. Nobody was allowed to work between 8:00 and 5:00. A, a car full of Gestapo men-- I forgot the name of the chief of the Gestapo lieutenant. They were driving in the cars, on the streets. And who was in the street, they shot him right away. So I was in the Jewish community, in the Judenrat there. Because Remba was there, a good friend of mine. So I was doing nothing there. I was just sitting there. And I was working, so-called-- I was working. I had the papers. I had to work for somebody. For whom did you work? For the Jewish-- for the Judenrat.

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I remember when I was-- at five after 5:00, I dared to go home. So I had to go with a big, you know, shovel, on my-- here. And that's how I did. I'll go to work, it looked like. But I saw them, the Gestapo. They didn't touch me. Now, October 2, 1942, I met one young man. He was in his early 20s, tall-- 6'1', 6'2'-- a powerful guy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I see him now. I talked to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I asked him, who are you? He says, I was in Treblinka. I said, what is Treblinka? The trains which were leaving Warsaw in the afternoon were coming early morning to Treblinka. It was a small station. There was a symphony orchestra playing beautiful music.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was an SS major with an angelic face-- and telling to the people who were coming out from the train, now your parasitic life is ended. You're going to work-- to work in the East. And you will live very comfortable. Everyone will have an apartment. You will have-- your children will go to school. But first, before you go, you have to take off your clothes, and go to-- for a shower. And then you go out and be loaded on the trains. Now, people jokingly and laughingly-- they didn't know-- they went into these showers. And on the other side, so-called Sonderkommando-- special Kommando, very strong boys-- were taking the corpses out and putting them in graves and burying them. Now this young man was working in a Sonderkommando too, because he was so powerful. But he was collecting clothes and putting the clothes on a train. And he hid. And he came to Warsaw. And he told us. He told me about this. Right away the whole-- the whole ghetto knew about it, what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] money-- we-- everybody was giving money to try to buy arms. Because, we said, we wouldn't go like this. So nothing specially was going on. We're buying some arms. But it was very difficult. Money was no problem. There were millions of dollars in the Warsaw ghetto and gold by tons. For a machine gun, we would play it-- we will pay a ton of-- a ton of gold.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So this all was going on until January 19, 1943. It was early morning. And we were doing on a young lady an abortion. It was my friend. He was doing the abortion, I was giving the anesthesia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And after we finished, I looked out the window. And I saw the black uniform. It was Ukrainian. I said to my friend, it's an action! Let's hid! Let's go somewhere!

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to the lady-- she came down to the table-- I said, come with me. She says, don't worry about me. She took out a little card, you know, a plastic card, like the credit card. She was an agent of the Gestapo, a Jewish girl. There were such, too, informers. Well, I went up. I don't know where he went. But I went up on an attic and stupidly enough, I just stood behind a door. And the Jewish police-- the policemen, I heard the Jewish policemen coming up. And one downstairs asked him, is there anybody there?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, no, there is nobody here. He urinated there. I almost stopped breathing because he was just behind the door. I stopped breathing. If he would have caught me, then I would have gone. This was the third miracle. Now, Mordechai Anielewicz did one mistake at that time. He took a group of his soldiers out into the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Jewish police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Jewish police-- out of his soldiers, you know, the underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: He took the underground soldiers into the street. Now, how could they stand in front of a regularly armed army? So it was a fight. He lost quite a few, the cream of the underground. And he went back. And he knew that there were only-- no open war, but only guerrilla warfare. But the action lasted only three days because the Germans never expected that the Jews-- the Untermenschen, as they called them, the lower people, the lower Menschen-- will dare to come out with guns against the super race. So the action lasted only three days, from this-- from January 19th until the 22nd. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, after the 22nd-- and we knew what's going on, where they go, to Treblinka, to death-- so buy, buy. But the Poles didn't want to sell us-- the army, the Polish Army. We paid for one revolver $3,000 to $5,000, for one bullet, $10. Now, during the main or so-called resettlement, there was a very complicated political situation in the Warsaw Ghetto. Because there were so many parties. And the, the, the, the Zionist, the Right and Left, and Revisionist, they couldn't get together. But on the 28th, they formed-- that somehow they came together. And they formed 22 groups. They called-- one was called ZOB, the Jewish Fighting Organization. And the other one was called Irgun Zvai Leumi. This was the Revisionist, which were the followers of Jabotinsky and Trumpeldor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what was the main thing now? Buy arms. Because they were-- the ZOB had 700 people. And Zvai Leumi had 300. But could you have thousands of armed people-- but there were no arms.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish government in London at that time sent us 50 revolvers with munition, 50 hand grenades, and 4 kilograms of explosives. We bought 4,000-- on the Aryan side for money-- 4,000 liters of gasoline. So when the tanks attack it, we burn it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, we trained the youngsters. As I said, we were preparing ourselves to fight. The, the underground, the 700 and the 300, they were trained. But what? We didn't have any guns, any-- what do you call it, these guns-- Rifles?

speaker: interviewee
text: --with sticks. Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: Bayonets and rifles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rifles. We didn't have rifles. So they trained with sticks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So there was a bunker. In every building, there was a bunker where people had to hid in case of a new action. And there was a physician assigned to every bunker. I was-- I had 70 or 80 people in the bunker, assigned to my bunker, from my building. And, oh, unforgettable-- the first night of Pesach, the first Seder-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but before I tell you about this, we had spies, Jewish spies on the Aryan side. And the German knew that they wouldn't get us out so simple. They'll have to fight. And they told us how they prepared, the German. What they had against us is 2,000 SS soldiers. Listen to this-- three detachments of artillery; one thousand of German police; one thousand of Polish police; and one thousand of Ukrainians, Lithuanians, Latvians, and Estonians in the black uniforms, the supporting police. Against them, we were 700 in the Jewish Fighting Organization and 300 in the other organization. But the Germans knew we will come-- we won't come easy. And on the first Seder, on April 19, 1943, we got a note-- calls from the other side. The Germans are surrounding the ghetto. So immediately, everybody went to the bunkers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And me, I have some medicine. What medicine did I have? Aspirin and nothing else, but-- and some material, you know, for worms. And all the soldiers went to their posts.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And on the 19th in the morning, I'll show you on that-- my drawings there-- through the Muranowska gate, next to the Umschlagplatz, a platoon of German soldiers came through the Muranowska Street into the ghetto, and went to Muranowska Square, singing, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Northern people, the Ubermenschen, Supermenschen-- singing, happy. Now, when they came in Muranowska Street, there were two buildings there with flags, one Jewish flag, with the Magen David, and one Polish flag. The boys-- the Jewish boys knew, that every bullet has to kill. Otherwise, they wouldn't come out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when they stopped singing and standing there on the Muranowska Square, they came under fire. And it was hell for them because so many fell. They threw their guns. And they ran away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now every piece of arms-- every revolver, every gun, every submachine gun-- were collected. That's how the-- how the underground got submachine guns. There was not one-- one was only in the whole ghetto. And uniforms-- everything was collected. Two tanks went on Muranowska-- on Zamenhofa Street, also from Muranowska Gate, on Zamenhofa Street-- I'll show it to you-- to Mila 8. And there, there were waiting for them two tanks. They threw the Molotov cocktails on them and burned two tanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the Germans left. They were scared. They left the ghetto. And they knew they can't fight just on open streets. So they sent planes. And the planes started to bomb us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, Himmler, Himmler was the Reichsfuhrer of the SS and Gestapo. He nominated a new general to the Gestapo. His name I will never forget because I saw him-- Jurgen von Stroop, General Jurgen von Stroop. And he gave him an order to liquidate the ghetto, make it 'Judenrein.' It mean 'free of Jews'-- as a birthday gift to Hitler on April 20. This was his birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: So April 19 is when we got this. And there was no birthday gift for him because it was-- fighting was going on, then the planes. And it didn't help. Fighting was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, they had to-- there was-- so organized, so well-organized, you know, that they broke down walls on the attics, so you could-- from one apartment-- from one building to another, from one side of the ghetto to the other side, you could give-- tell them what to do. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was April 19th. Oh, I forgot to tell you about Lejkin. I told you that Lejkin was such a gangster. In February of 1942, he was shot by the Jewish underground. That's what he should have. He received what, what he should have had.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was the smuggler?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was the second after Szerynski. He was walking around with that-- with that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whip?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whip. And beating Jews, and screaming, and beating them. He thought that he's a big shot. So they killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jew, yeah, sure. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jurgen von Stroop, General Jurgen von Stroop, what to do with the Jews? So he gave an order to burn the ghetto, the whole ghetto. And one house next to the other was burned. Now, thousands of people with children perished in the fires. And I was in the bunker. And my house was burning. We couldn't stay there. And we came out. It was April 29, 1943. And just imagine, on the street, thousands of people standing there. And every three yards was a machine gun with two soldiers, towards us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I had in my pocket a bottle of water. Jurgen von Stroop came by. He looked at it. He said, throw it down. I threw it down. Now, this face I'll never forget in my life. He was short stature, with a big red nose, a drinker, probably-- ugly guy. Next to me was standing a friend of mine, probably 6'2', 6'3'-- tall. And the soldier said to him, that Jew, I saw him shooting our soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave only one order. [GERMAN]! It means 'shoot him.' He had to come out under the wall, bullet into the skull. That--
